input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation employs a multi‑tool MBSE approach. System structure and behavior are modeled with SysML (including v1.7 IBDs and state‑machine diagrams) and architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. MontiArc Automaton extensions add run‑time behavior components, while state machines in SysML v1.7 capture discrete logic. The modeling workflow combines several methodologies—RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT—to manage abstraction layers from requirements down to product/technical design. Simulation and analysis are supported by MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools and methods, and general‑purpose programming in C++ or Java is used for code generation and execution of behavioral models. No database or persistence technology is referenced in the provided excerpt.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital shadow operates in real‑time, continuously ingesting live sensor time‑series from the physical asset via MQTT and immediately mapping them to the in‑memory cosim model representation. The model update loop is triggered by incoming measurement events, but the document does not specify mandatory update frequencies, maximum latencies, or temporal granularity for specific services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows a Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) expressed as a UML class diagram, integrating three core layers: Asset (physical descriptors), Model (formal SysML/ADL architecture), and Processing (runtime logic). The processing layer applies state‑machine models (MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7) and interfaces with simulation engines such as MathWorks Simulink or compiled C++/Java code. Component composition and interconnection are defined using architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) in compliance with MBSE best practices, enabling resource management via component instantiation semantics and system‑wide coordination through the processing engine dispatching sensor events to the digital model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Model components are expressed using MBSE techniques such as SysML (inter‑block diagrams, state charts) and architecture description languages like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, often supplemented with simulation models in MathWorks Simulink or compiled code in C++/Java. These models capture the system’s structural composition (components and connectors) and behavioral dynamics (state machines or automata), and may be combined across multiple notation layers to support visualization, simulation and analysis of the Digital Twin constellations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: All sensor data from the physical robot is captured as continuous time‑series streams and sent to the digital twin via an MQTT broker. The messages contain the raw sensor measurements and associated timestamps. The digital twin receives these MQTT payloads, performing minimal preprocessing (e.g., timestamp concatenation, data type conversion) before aggregating them into contextual data traces for downstream analysis and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The communication between the physical robot and the digital twin is performed over MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) using the standard TCP/IP stack. Sensor streams are published by the robot to MQTT topics; the digital twin subscribes to these topics to receive the data. No specific details about QoS level, TLS encryption, broker topology, or network bandwidth are specified.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The document describes Digital Twin engineering as following Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for layered modeling (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). It references several methodologies to manage complexity, including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) employed are ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML Interface Block Diagrams, which model component‑connector structures and, in the case of MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, provide behavioral descriptions. Simulation and analysis are supported by MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools, while implementation may use general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. The text notes integration across heterogeneous stakeholders, but does not specify version control, quality assurance, testing or formal evolution‑management procedures.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the document explicitly identifies the system in Section III as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes'. It further details the 'first vertical robot axis' as the focus, including components like the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor' and 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', yet the Abstract and Section IV describe services such as 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' and 'continuous... identification of joint friction'. The purpose is explicitly defined as the 'understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'.

The extraction incorrectly claims the implementation uses tools like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and methodologies like RFLP. The source text in Section II.A lists these only as general examples ('e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc'), whereas the actual implementation in Section IV utilizes 'SysML' and 'MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'. The extraction also misses Asset Administration Shells (AAS) mentioned in the Abstract.

It accurately notes the absence of specific update frequencies. The score is 4 because phrases like 'in-memory cosim model representation' are interpretative and not explicitly in the text.

The document focuses on a single digital shadow instance for a specific robot axis and does not describe multiple instances, hierarchies, or coordination between twins. The 'Not in Document' claim is accurate. Supported by quotes: 'The class FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow.', 'focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100... The first vertical robot axis... is used for creating a digital shadow.'

The extraction is highly accurate and specific. It correctly describes the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a UML class diagram integrating Asset, Model, and Processing layers, which is directly supported by the text: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram'. The mention of architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and simulation engines (MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java) is explicitly supported by the Background section: 'ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26]... MathWorks Simulink [28]... C++ or Java'. The description of state-machine models is also supported: 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7'. The extraction captures the technical depth of the system orchestration described in the source.

The document focuses on the internal data flow between a single physical robot and its digital shadow (e.g., via MQTT from sensors). It does not describe integration with external systems, interoperability standards with other enterprise systems, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins. The mention of AAS is implementation-focused rather than describing external integration patterns. The 'Not in Document' claim is accurate. Supported by quotes: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.', 'A digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object”'.

Therefore, the extraction is accurate and specific but includes some technical details not found in the text.

The text notes that a digital shadow 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.' There is no mention of control flows, commands, or feedback from digital to physical, so 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately describes the communication protocol as MQTT and mentions the data flow mechanism. The extraction also correctly notes the absence of specific details like QoS, TLS, or broker topology in the source. The detail about 'standard TCP/IP stack' is a reasonable inference for MQTT but not explicitly stated in the text. Overall, the description is specific, technically accurate, and well-grounded in the provided sources.

The document does not provide information about hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational and storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability/reliability assurance. It only mentions a dashboard for visualization without specifying the underlying deployment infrastructure. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document describes a methodology for creating a Digital Shadow (steps 1-4) and discusses modeling layers (RFLP), but it does not describe the lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin itself, such as operation, maintenance, disposal, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches. The characteristic definition requires a description of the twin's lifecycle over time, which is absent.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the engineering processes described in Section II.A and II.C. It correctly lists the MBSE approach, SysML, specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and simulation tools (Simulink). It accurately notes the absence of detailed quality assurance, testing, and version control procedures, as the text only mentions traceability. Supported by quotes: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'methodologies... RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... SMArDT', 'Architecture description languages (ADLs)... ACME, AADL, MontiArc... SysML IBDs'.

While the document mentions the goal of 'understanding' friction and improving accuracy, as well as a 'dashboard' for visualization, it does not describe the specific decision support capabilities, decision making processes, analytics processing capabilities, or confidence measures required by the characteristic definition. The text focuses on the construction of the shadow rather than the decision-making framework.

The text mentions the use of standards like SysML, UML, and specifications like Asset Administration Shells (AAS) as tools or technologies. However, it does not provide a description of standards compliance, certification requirements, compliance frameworks, or interoperability standards governance. The characteristic is focused on the formal aspects of standardization, which are not detailed in the text.
